architecture.summary: PICARD is an inference-time constrained decoding method that wraps any auto-regressive language model decoder. At each generation step, it receives the model's log-softmax scores over vocabulary and the partial hypothesis token sequence. PICARD restricts scoring to the top-k highest-probability tokens, then applies incremental SQL parsing (via monadic parser combinators) to reject inadmissible tokens by setting their scores to negative infinity. The method operates on the detokenized surface form of SQL and has access to schema metadata (table/column names). Four parsing modes—lexing, parsing-without-guards, parsing-with-guards, and off—control the stringency of constraint checking. Accepted tokens retain their model score; rejected tokens are masked from beam search. PICARD is entirely inference-time; the base model is frozen and unmodified.

biblio.title: PICARD: Parsing Incrementally for Constrained Auto-Regressive Decoding from Language Models

claims.statement: On Spider development set, T5-3B+Picard achieves exact-set-match accuracy of 75.5% and execution accuracy of 79.3%
On Spider test set, T5-3B+Picard achieves exact-set-match accuracy of 71.9% and execution accuracy of 75.1%
On CoSQL dialog state tracking development set, T5-3B+Picard achieves question match accuracy of 56.9% and interaction match accuracy of 24.2%
With Picard parsing with guards at top-2, T5-3B reduces invalid SQL predictions from 12% to 2% on Spider development set
Decoding speed on T5-3B with beam size 4 on NVIDIA A100 GPU: 2.5 seconds per sample without Picard, 3.1 seconds per sample with Picard

PICARD is compatible with any existing auto-regressive language model decoder and vocabulary, does not require custom control tokens or model architecture, and can be easily enabled or disabled at inference time without modifying the pre-trained model

PICARD operates through four parsing modes of increasing stringency: lexing (lexical checking), parsing-without-guards (grammatical checking), parsing-with-guards (semantic binding enforcement), and off (no checking); each higher mode subsumes all constraints of lower modes
Without Picard, these T5 models do not benefit from beam search; performance is largely independent of beam size in lexing mode
